speaker: interviewer
text: Today is June 4, 1996. The survivor being interviewed is Eva Newman, born Eva Birenbaum. The interviewer is Benjamin Weiner. We are in New York, New York in the United States of America. And this interview will be conducted in English. Today is June 4, 1996. This is Eva Newman, born Eva Birenbaum. My name is Benjamin Weiner. We're in New York, New York in the United States of America. And this interview will be conducted in English. Could you please say your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eva Neumann.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eva Birenbaum.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have a Hebrew name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Tzipporah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you go by any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have a nickname?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Madie.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who calls you Madie.

speaker: interviewee
text: Only people knew me before the war, my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not my family. Not my present family. My past family from before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zigmund [BACKGROUND NOISE] Birenbaum.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he have a Hebrew name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Yehoshua. And what was your mother's name? Margaret Pollack.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she have a Hebrew name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Devorah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had two brothers. One was Szmul Birenbaum, and the other one was Isaac Birenbaum.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: 8th of March, 1929.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your brothers older than you or younger than you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they were 15 months younger and another 15 months younger.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were born in '30--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In [? Szolyva. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what country was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was born, it was Czechoslovakia. Previously it was Hungary, and then it became Hungary again. And then, now it's Russia. [SIGHS] I don't know what it is. It's Ukraine, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is the town located geographically?

speaker: interviewee
text: Geographically, the next big town was Munkatsch. And it was on the border of Czechoslovakia, Hungary, whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was in the mountains?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Carpathian Mountains, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your parents from the same town?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was born there. My grandfather came from Poland somewhere in the 18th century before my father was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the 18th or in the 19th century? In the 1800s?

speaker: interviewee
text: 18th because my father was born in the 18th century, end of the 18th. And I think he was one of the first Jews who came to-- who came over the border. And that's where he stopped. And he established himself there, and that's how the family tree started. That's where we started.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of this grandfather?

speaker: interviewee
text: Szmul Birenbaum.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what brought him over the border?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think the troubles in-- in Poland, Russia. I don't quite know his particularly reason, but must have been pograms already then, and that's how he came over.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he left Poland because of the pograms. And then he came over to the Austro-Hungarian empire.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. Yeah. And he established himself there and he built a family. And actually the town was a very small town, and there were no Jews. So I think he was one of the, or he was the first Jew. And I don't know why he just stopped there. You know, you never know once you come over the border-- if your energy or money or whatever runs out, and you stop. And he stopped. But the real story, the real reason, the details, I wouldn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: When approximately was your father born?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was born in 1894. And he-- he was born in this town. But my grandfather was married before my father's mother. And he had quite a big family. So there was a-- I had an uncle who was about 45 years older than my father from the first marriage. There were a lot of children between the four marriages because my grandmother was married before, and then they had children together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did all these families live in the same town in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, I-- I-- I still remember the time when I was a child, I saw that everybody's family name was Birenbaum. It took me a long-- I was quite an older, bigger child when I realized that there were other people [LAUGHS] living there. I think there were about 320 families in this little town. And I think later on when they started getting married that I realized that there were other people there, not just Birenbaums. Why it's so interesting because I don't know any relation of-- under Birenbaum anywhere in the world, any more. There were a few other ones straight after the war. And I lost touch although one of them changed their name [INAUDIBLE] all around the world is like being in touch with the-- that immediate but they weren't called Birenbaum. My two aunts who got married-- one was a Rosenbaum and one was a Mammerstein, but not Birenbaum.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your grandfather came to the town as the only Jew, but he apparently done his best to people the town with Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well, he started, but I don't know if he came married then-- these children or he came as a-- I don't know. That I wouldn't-- couldn't tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the composition of the town approximately when you were born in terms of Jews and non-Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: See, it was a town. The town itself was small, but it had a lot of little villages which was like 10 minutes' walk. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think quite a big proportion was Jewish. What the proportion was, I don't know. I don't know. You know, when you live in a town like that, you don't much have to do as a child with the other part. Later on, when the anti-Semitism started, there were very noisy. Then it looked like they were more than we were. You know, it's-- I can only tell you it was in a child's mind. I was a child then. I can only tell you what I saw. I don't want to interpret it what I see now. But remembering that I thought everybody was more or less Jewish, which was like, for instance, anybody who worked in our land or in our business, all the non-Jewish people spoke Yiddish. So, you know, so we didn't, as children, we didn't know there were other. But when we started going to school, they started abusing us. And then things started, and we realized it wasn't just Birenbaum and just Jews. There were other people there.

speaker: interviewer
text: But in-- in your early life, or when you were very young, it felt like you were living within a Jewish community.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. Yes, yes. We didn't have much to do with anybody else. And we lived in that town. There were very few non-Jews lived in the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, many of the non-Jews lived in the-- in the smaller villages.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, even Jews lived in smaller places except all these places like for Jewish prayer, you need 10 people. So we had two shuls in town, and-- and all these people from around, surrounding towns used to come in to pray in our-- in town because--

speaker: interviewer
text: What other kinds of Jewish officials did you have in the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, oh, you mean Jewish-- yes, yes. There was-- there was every-- you know, one of everything. There was a rabbi and a shaliach and a chazan and there was a lot of Jewish teachers. There was a yeshiva there. And lots of people came from far to go to this yeshiva. And because these boys-- there was no food in the yeshivas. So they used to-- you know, the custom was every boy had a home, every day another home. So we had this, used to be called Take-- you know, 'take' really literally means 'taste.' And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So your family would put up yeshiva boys they came to the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they could sleep in the yeshiva, but they didn't have food.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they fed them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah, yeah, they came to us to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the yeshiva boys that would come through?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but some of them were family, you know, like relations who lived far, further away, came to the yeshiva there to learn there. It was a great big yeshiva. It was mainly for younger-- younger boys.

words.text: It was a great big yeshiva.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it wasn't a very large town, but it seems like it was--

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't a large but very concentrated, you know. I mean, it's a Jewish town. It was-- but I think all those towns were more or less the same type, or at least we-- us children were kept in this ghetto kind of existence. I think it still exists, you know. People keep to them, in their own surrounding and their own circle.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say ghetto--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's-- ghetto-- I've never heard the word before. I mean, if you're an observant Jew, you've got to live within-- within the community where you have got the kosher food and the shul and then all the other. So there was very concentrated in Jews as probably is today the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father a religious man?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, very. Very. I-- I don't think there are many non-religious in town. I don't think there was a choice. I do remember there was one family who didn't keep anything, were completely non-- and-- and we looked upon them as some-- something out of a novel. [SIGHS] We-- we just-- we couldn't understand it. Probably there were people who weren't so religious. But it was not officially [LAUGHS] acknowledged. But it was, where I lived, it was a Hasidishly-inclined town. Everybody was Hasidish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father was Hasidish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was his rebbe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you think I remember his name? The nice old man with the long beard.

speaker: interviewer
text: But which-- which Hasidic rebbe did he-- did he follow?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father followed the Belzer. He used to go to Belz a lot. And Belzer rebbe used to come to us because, really, where we lived was in the mountains. And we had a lot of mineral baths and mineral supplements, very healthy, like a holiday place. And they used to stay with us and take the baths. Was cold, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see the Belzer rebbe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. But I don't know. I can't quite now see which generation. I know he was an old man and used to come to us and sit at the top of the table. And all the Hasidim came Friday night, and it was very jolly and exciting. And I had become very important because all my friends said, oh, the rabbi is staying there, you know. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was an honor.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a great honor.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a great, big honor, yes. Yes. It was very exciting times. I mean, it wasn't exciting for me, but the atmosphere was exciting. It was still a little bit peaceful, and you didn't feel the war that much. I was-- I was, as I said, I was very young, so everything looked different.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else do you remember about your father's religious practice? What other kinds of things did he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: He used to get up very, very early in the morning. The custom was you got up very, very early, and you went to the beis medrash, like in the shul. And you sat and learned. And then you davened. You didn't come home between. And-- and then he went to work. And then he used to go back to the beis medrash. Everything was around the shul and the beis medrash. It was davening and learning in between and davening again and the learning. And it was very, very strict. And as they say, schlepped my brothers with. And, you know, it was a very cold country. It was very, very. bitter cold, and you had to get up in it very early in the morning. That was part of life. You didn't question it or criticize it or-- that was how it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your father celebrate the Shabbos?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very seriously. [LAUGHS] And it was a-- it was a-- in a small town, Shabbos was the event of the week. And-- and you prepared for it starting on Wednesday and the baking and the cooking and the preparation and-- and because of there were so many people coming from the surrounding towns, we always provided kiddish after shul before they went home because they always had to walk a long time. And all these [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Hatsidish gentlemen used to come and have kiddish in our house and then walk back. That was the-- and then we used to go to my older aunt, was older than my-- my father. And, you know, it was stretched out, the Shabbat. Then we came back and had lunch and sleeping and then learning, learning, learning. That was the-- my father and my brothers, there was-- was always a book open and was learning. That was the main-- that's how I think everybody did it. I mean, as a child, you think whatever you do, the whole world does the same. But there was nothing much else to do there. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, what kind of foods did you eat on Shabbat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the usual-- fish and-- it was a big event, the fish, because if the river was frozen, you couldn't fish, and that was tragedy. So they used to kind of open a tin of sardine and the chicken-- chicken soup.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was Friday evening meal?

speaker: interviewee
text: Friday evening meal.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about Saturday afternoon?

speaker: interviewee
text: Saturday afternoon, we had the cholent which was taken to the baker to be cooked. You prepared it in a big pan, and you take it to the baker.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what was in the cholent?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was beans, barley, and all the bits and pieces from chickens and meats. And it was delicious. I'm sure I couldn't eat it today, but then [LAUGHTER] it was delicious. And it was the atmosphere. You know, after shul everybody taking their children home. And-- and, you know, if somebody wasn't on time, we used to wrap it in blankets. It shouldn't catch cold. And it was very, very exciting. And looking back on it, it was very primitive. But it was part of life. We never saw that we are short or it's wrong or it's-- that was the way we lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you mean primitive when you look back at it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, today, you put it in the crock pot, or you put it in your own oven. You don't possibly [INTERPOSING VOICES] go to the baker. [LAUGHS] And it wasn't looked up on primitive then. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the way of life?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the way of life, yes. And as a child, everything is wonderful as long as everybody else does it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what Yom Kippur was like in the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very solemn, very quiet like you could drop a pin on the street. You couldn't-- you know, it was really very, very, very solemn, very serious. And we used to go and visit our parents in shul, and millions of candles, a whole sun pit in the entry of the shul. And a guard that no children should go in and disturb the davening. And it was very serious, very, very serious.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the shul like?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were two shuls. There was a very, very old one. It was really more for the older people. Then there was a new one just called the-- the shul. It was-- was a gallery, and children weren't allowed because there was never enough room. When the boys-- the boys were more allowed. The girls were definitely not allowed to go to shul. And it was like any altar of shul. I mean, even today they look exactly the same. And it was taken very holy, very seriously, the shul, you know. That was the focal point of-- of the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there seats for women in the shul?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there seats for women in the shul?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, upstairs there was. Yes, but never enough for children. It was the thing children just did not go to shul. And we all used to meet them and wait for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how would you spend Shabbos if you weren't in shul?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were children. We were playing outside. We had to daven because it was checked by somebody or other always managed to check. But we davened. But we davened because it was-- we didn't-- we davened. That's it. So all the kids used to daven together, and then we played. And-- we walked to the shul to wait for our parents. And then we came home and served all of these people different kind of foods before they went. And then the excitement was going to my aunt, and you were waiting to be invited to stay. And that was the big treat. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was that the big treat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I was the only daughter at home, and I was always bored. And they had eight daughters, so it was always a treat for me to stay. And I stayed quite a lot there. It was more exciting. And they lived like a little south of town. It was like a country house. And there was much more to do there than in our house which was in the middle of town. So it was very exciting.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you spend your time with their cousins? With all the girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we used to have games. And, you know, there were so many of us then, and, you know, there was always something to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of games would you play?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had games. We had certain games, and we made up a lot of games. You know, if you don't put a child in front of the television, they would make up a game. We did make up games. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any examples of a game that you made up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we used to-- everything was mountain. Whatever you did, you have to climb. The house was the bottom, and then you just climbed. So we used to climb and come down and roll down and, you know. We used to make our own toys from boxes and hide and seek, you know, this kind of games.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was a little bit outside of the center of the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my aunt lived a little bit out. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the geography like? What did it look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was a long main street. And there was a town hall in the middle. We lived up a little of the town hall. And then houses on both sides. And then from the main street, there were little tiny streets off. And my aunt lived at the end of this main road plus a bridge and then-- like we were really allowed to carry in town but not to my aunt. The bridge, you have to-- couldn't carry from that any more. They lived a little bit outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean on Shabbos, you could carry inside the town. That was an area.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. That's how time passed. And that's-- these are the things I remember for till about 1939, '40. And then, things started to go bad. You weren't allowed this, and you weren't allowed that. And you weren't allowed to go to this school. And problems and there was-- we felt the vibration of something going wrong. You never knew what, but you've heard from your parents and worries and abuses in school. So and then you weren't allowed to go to this school. You are only allowed to go to that school. And then schools used to change from Czech to Polish to Russian to Ukraine. By the time you've finished one language for one week, it has changed. Another precedent came in and-- And then there was also we weren't allowed to go to the Hebrew school because it wasn't Hasidish enough. So we created our own little Jewish school. Education, I don't think we managed a lot because there was no stability. We weren't long enough in a class or long enough-- like, for my cousins who were older than I was, they had proper education. But we had to struggle. I mean, we had some education, [BACKGROUND NOISE] but we had to struggle. And going to school was physical pain because you had thrown, winter, is snowballs and, summer, was stones. And you are the Jew. It was-- nobody objected. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you begin to go to school? At what age?

speaker: interviewee
text: Five. The boys used to go to Cheder from the age of three and the girls from five.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went to Cheder.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not me. The boys went from the age of three. And we went to school at the age of five.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of school was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- I started in a Czech elementary school, which was still wonderful. Czechs-- Czech-- Czech people themselves are very, very nice people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their good qualities?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What are the good qualities that they had?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were very tolerant. They weren't anti-Semitic. They were more refined. They were-- they were a better class to the people who, you know, the surrounding people there. They-- they are more educated. And they ran big institutes. They weren't anti-Semitic. They-- they were-- they were very, very kind to us. Of course, they were also limited for what they were allowed to do because officially, they took your business away, they took your land away. And-- so they couldn't do much. And then 1939, they just had to leave. And that I remember was very sad because I had some Czech friends. And it was very, very bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these friends that you'd made in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the school like? The Czech school that you started at at five?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, it was not class 1, class 2. Usually in one class, you had three-- three classes in one room. And yes, that was still fine at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was learning like there? What was the pattern of education?

speaker: interviewee
text: The pattern of education was repetition. You have to, you know, everything had to be by heart and repeated and repeated. And spelling was repeated and repeated until you knew it. Of course, don't forget that in this class could be five, six people with different languages. So you had to learn it by heart because otherwise you couldn't cope with the class. There were children that only spoke Russian, children that only spoke Czech, children who only spoke Yiddish, and children who only spoke Hungarian because my parents spoke Hungarian at home because my mother always-- her mother language was Hungarian.

speaker: interviewer
text: So education-- you know, the teacher had a job. So everything was learned by heart. But I think in those days, it was fashionable to learn things by heart. But in this case, it was a necessity.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I don't think it stayed long enough. You know, that-- that kind of education doesn't remain with you because it changes so often that it's very hard to remember what, you know-- your memories should be from what you learned as a child. Our memory isn't because it wasn't solid enough. And then-- then we went-- then after 1939, we weren't allowed to go to any non-Jewish schools. So we created a school, a Jewish school, so-called. And then, every Jewish child to age of 10 was in the same room, in the same class, divided within the class into age groups.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that school held? Where were classes?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a special-- there was a special building which was one big room [BACKGROUND NOISE] in a very big garden. It was-- it was OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who organized it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably the town, you know, the Jewish people who-- and the boys used to go to the Cheders, so that wasn't such a big problem. It was only problem when they started cutting their hair off and peyas off and, you know, and they are abused and so-- it's-- there are some nice memories, till about 1939. After that, I don't even remember anything bad happening. It was just the atmosphere of-- of the life in the town which changed.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe the atmosphere?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anxious. Terribly anxious. And as a child you feel the vibration of your parents' anxiety. Shall we go here? Shall we go there? Shall we run away? How can you run away? Where will we run? You know, it was this kind of all the time and-- and don't stay out late. And if-- and you knew if you were five minutes late, your parents were going mad because they were worried who attacked you on the street. And to us it's like today, if you can't come home, your mother is worried. I just thought my mother was worried, but I felt it was more than that. So you know the atmosphere was getting very, very bad. And then, 1942 my father was called up. It wasn't called up. It was a-- they collected from the town anybody who was anybody. Either you were a lawyer or a solicitor or a doctor. They collected all the-- and my-- my father was doing something in the town hall, whatever. And they collected them and put them in to working. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they were treated very badly.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was by the Hungarians.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was by the Hungarians, and the people who were above them were the gypsies. They were the guards, and they were the bosses. It was very, very, very-- that was the first humiliating part of the town when that happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was that humiliating?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, these gypsies [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] weren't educated or anything. And they kind of took revenge. Here were these so-called rich people who-- whom we were always under or-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and now we could take revenge. Like you had an old-- we had an old doctor, a little fat, old man. And they used to make him carry two sacks at a time. You will carry it. And if not, they hit him. You know, it-- it was very humiliating. And that was the first of the down, the road down, the way down. That was the very bad. They were there for a few months and--

speaker: interviewer
text: We're just going to pause now and change the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
